problem.text: Given an array of integers nums and an integer k, find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of exactly size k.

keywords.explanation: adjacent elements only — no skipping
fixed window — never shrinks or grows
the requirement: what you must optimise for

missingConstraints: Can nums contain negative integers?
Can nums be empty or null?
Is k guaranteed to be ≤ nums.length?

concepts.definition: A slice where elements are adjacent in index order — no skipping. Must be a consecutive run from the original array.

easy_medium.text: Sort the array, then use binary search to find the max window
easy_medium.explanation: Sorting destroys positional order — 'contiguous' means index-adjacency matters, so sorting immediately invalidates the approach.

easy_medium.text: Two nested loops — outer sets the start index, inner sums k elements
easy_medium.explanation: Correct but O(n·k). The sliding window reuses the previous window sum to get O(n) — no reason to recompute from scratch each time.

easy_medium.text: Sliding window — add the right element, subtract the left element as the window moves
easy_medium.explanation: Fixed-size window: one addition and one subtraction per step. O(n) time, O(1) space.

cards.sayApproach: The key words are 'contiguous' and 'exactly size k' — contiguous means index-order matters so sorting is out, and the fixed size means I never need to grow or shrink conditionally. I'll use a fixed sliding window: add the right element on every step, and once the window reaches size k I record the max and subtract the leftmost element. One pass — O(n) time, O(1) space. Before I code: can nums contain negative integers? Is k guaranteed to be ≤ nums.length?

lines.narration: My function takes the array and window size k
maxSum = MIN_VALUE — correctly handles all-negative inputs
Pattern: expand right — add the incoming element
Constraint k: only act once the window is full
Requirement: track the maximum sum seen
Pattern: shrink left — drop the oldest element

cards.verbalSummary: Single-pass fixed sliding window. I expand right by adding each element, and once the window fills to size k I record the max and shrink left by subtracting the oldest element. Time O(n), space O(1). Key edge case: I initialised maxSum to Integer.MIN_VALUE rather than zero — this correctly handles arrays where all values are negative.
solRatingCriteria: Identified 'contiguous' + 'fixed size k' as the sliding window signal
Explained why sorting invalidates the contiguous constraint
Stated O(n) time and O(1) space with justification
Mentioned Integer.MIN_VALUE edge case for all-negative arrays

modifications.question: What if k is not fixed — find the longest subarray with sum ≤ target?
modifications.approach: Switch to a variable window: expand right freely, shrink from the left while sum exceeds target. Track max length (right - left + 1) instead of max sum. The shrink condition changes from a size check to a value check.
modifications.codeChunk:
while (windowSum > target) windowSum -= nums[left++];
maxLen = Math.max(maxLen, right - left + 1);

modifications.question: Return the start and end indices of the max-sum window, not the sum.
modifications.approach: Track a bestStart variable alongside maxSum. When updating maxSum, record i - k + 1 as bestStart. Return int[] { bestStart, bestStart + k - 1 }.
modifications.codeChunk:
if (windowSum > maxSum) { maxSum = windowSum; bestStart = i - k + 1; }
return new int[]{ bestStart, bestStart + k - 1 };

modifications.question: The input is now a 2D matrix — find the k×k submatrix with maximum sum.
modifications.approach: Precompute row-prefix sums so any row-range sum is O(1). Then slide a window of k rows, using column prefix sums to reduce each column strip to a 1D sliding window problem. O(n²) overall.

problem.text: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

keywords.explanation: maximise length — variable window
the window validity condition

missingConstraints: Is the input lowercase only, or any Unicode/ASCII?
Can s be empty?

concepts.definition: A substring is contiguous — consecutive characters in order. A subsequence can skip characters. This problem says 'substring' so characters must be adjacent.

easy_medium.text: Generate all substrings with two nested loops, check each for uniqueness
easy_medium.explanation: O(n²) or O(n³) — correct but far too slow. The sliding window solves this in O(n).

easy_medium.text: Sliding window with a HashSet — shrink window when a duplicate enters
easy_medium.explanation: Expand right freely; when a duplicate appears, remove chars from the left until the window is valid again. O(n) amortised.

easy_medium.text: Sort the string and scan for the longest run of unique chars
easy_medium.explanation: Sorting destroys substring order — 'longest substring' is position-dependent, so sorting immediately breaks the problem.

cards.sayApproach: The condition 'without repeating characters' tells me the window is valid as long as all chars are unique — that's a variable window. I'll expand right, and whenever I see a duplicate I shrink from the left until the window is clean again. A HashSet lets me check membership and remove in O(1). Overall O(n) since each character enters and exits the window at most once.

lines.narration: Constraint: track uniqueness — HashSet for O(1) lookup
Constraint violated: duplicate found — shrink left
Remove leftmost char and advance left pointer

cards.verbalSummary: Variable sliding window with a HashSet. Expand right freely; when a duplicate enters shrink from the left. Each character enters and exits the window at most once so time is O(n). Space is O(min(n, charset size)) — bounded by the size of the alphabet.
solRatingCriteria: Identified 'without repeating' as a variable window validity condition
Chose HashSet for O(1) membership check and removal
Stated O(n) time with the 'each char enters/exits once' justification
Noted space depends on character set size, not just n

modifications.question: What if the string can have at most k replacements — find the longest substring with at most k distinct characters?
modifications.approach: Replace the HashSet with a HashMap tracking character frequencies. Shrink left when map.size() > k (too many distinct). The window is valid as long as distinct count ≤ k.

modifications.question: Find the longest substring where you can replace at most k characters to make all chars the same.
modifications.approach: Track frequency of the most common character in the window. If (windowSize - maxFreq) > k, shrink. This is Leetcode 424.

problem.text: Given an array of positive integers nums and a positive integer target, return the minimal length of a contiguous subarray whose sum is greater than or equal to target. Return 0 if no such subarray exists.

keywords.explanation: requirement: minimise the window size
variable window — indices must be adjacent
the window validity condition — shrink once met
key constraint — allows shrinking to always work

missingConstraints: Are all integers guaranteed positive? (critical — negative values break this approach)
What if no valid subarray exists — return 0 or -1?

concepts.definition: With all-positive values, adding more elements always increases the sum and removing always decreases it. This monotonic property is what makes the sliding window work — if the sum drops below target after shrinking, expanding right will always recover it.
concepts.example:
If nums had negatives, shrinking might accidentally increase the sum,
breaking the shrink-when-valid logic entirely → would need different approach

easy_medium.text: Prefix sums + binary search for minimum valid window
easy_medium.explanation: O(n log n) — works, but the variable sliding window is O(n). Binary search is unnecessary when the monotonic property holds.

easy_medium.text: Variable sliding window — expand right until sum ≥ target, then shrink left to minimise
easy_medium.explanation: O(n) — expand to reach target, record length, then shrink to find if a shorter valid window exists. Positive values guarantee monotonic behaviour.

easy_medium.text: Sort and use two pointers from both ends
easy_medium.explanation: Sorting destroys index order — 'contiguous subarray' is a positional constraint.

cards.sayApproach: All values are positive — that's the key. Because adding elements always increases the sum and removing always decreases it, I can use a variable window: expand right until sum reaches target, then record the length and shrink from the left to see if a shorter window is still valid. O(n) since each element enters and exits the window at most once.

lines.narration: Function takes the array and target
minLen = MAX_VALUE — handle the 'no valid window' case
Constraint met — window is valid, try to shrink
Pattern: shrink left to find shorter valid window
Requirement: return 0 if no valid window found

cards.verbalSummary: Variable sliding window relying on the all-positive guarantee. Expand right until sum ≥ target, then shrink left while valid to find the minimum length. O(n) — each element enters and exits the window at most once. Initialise minLen to MAX_VALUE and return 0 if it never updates.
solRatingCriteria: Identified the all-positive constraint as what makes sliding window viable
Explained the expand-then-shrink logic for minimisation
Stated O(n) with the 'each element enters/exits once' reasoning
Handled the no-valid-window edge case with MAX_VALUE sentinel

modifications.question: What if nums can contain negative integers?
modifications.approach: Sliding window breaks with negatives. Use prefix sums + monotonic deque for O(n), or prefix sums + binary search for O(n log n). The deque maintains indices of increasing prefix sums for efficient range queries.

problem.text: Given a string s and integer k, return the length of the longest substring containing at most k distinct characters.

keywords.explanation: variable window — maximise length
window validity: distinct character count ≤ k

missingConstraints: Is the character set lowercase only, or full ASCII/Unicode?
Is k = 0 a valid input — should empty string be returned?
Can s be empty?

concepts.definition: 'At most k distinct' means the window may contain up to k unique character types — but any count of each. It does NOT limit the total number of characters.
concepts.example:
k = 2
"aabbb"  → distinct: {a, b} = 2  ✓  valid
"aaabbc" → distinct: {a, b, c} = 3  ✗  too many
"aaaaa"  → distinct: {a} = 1  ✓  valid (under limit)

easy_medium.text: Variable sliding window with HashMap tracking char frequencies — shrink when distinct count exceeds k
easy_medium.explanation: HashMap.size() directly gives the number of distinct characters. Remove key when its count reaches 0. O(n) time, O(k) space.

easy_medium.text: Variable sliding window with int[26] frequency array (assuming lowercase)
easy_medium.explanation: Valid only if the interviewer confirmed lowercase ASCII. Without that confirmation, HashMap is the safer general answer. Always confirm the character set first.

easy_medium.text: Sort the string, find the longest run of ≤ k distinct chars
easy_medium.explanation: Sorting destroys positional order — 'substring' requires consecutive characters by original index.

cards.sayApproach: Asking first: is this lowercase only, or any character set? The window is valid as long as distinct character count ≤ k. I'll use a HashMap — its size directly tells me the distinct count. Expand right freely; when the map has more than k entries, shrink from the left, removing the count and deleting the key when it reaches zero. O(n) time, O(k) space.

lines.narration: Constraint: track distinct chars — HashMap size = distinct count
Add right char to frequency map
Constraint: too many distinct chars — shrink
Remove key when count hits zero — keeps size accurate

cards.verbalSummary: Variable sliding window using a HashMap where the map size gives the distinct character count. Expand right; shrink left when size exceeds k, removing the key entirely when its count reaches zero. O(n) time, O(k) space — the map never holds more than k+1 entries.
solRatingCriteria: Asked about character set before choosing data structure
Used HashMap.size() as the distinct count — not a separate counter
Removed key when count reaches zero to keep size accurate
Stated O(k) space rather than O(n)

modifications.question: Now find the longest substring with exactly k distinct characters.
modifications.approach: atMostK(s, k) - atMostK(s, k-1). The difference gives exactly-k. Reuse the at-most-k function. This is a standard trick.

problem.text: Given a string s and a non-empty string p, find all the start indices of p's anagrams in s. Return them in any order.

keywords.explanation: same characters, same frequency, any order
collect all valid window positions — not just one
window is exactly p.length() — fixed sliding window

missingConstraints: Are both strings guaranteed non-empty?
Is the character set lowercase only?

concepts.definition: Two strings are anagrams if they have the same characters with the same frequencies. The order doesn't matter. Comparing frequency maps is the standard O(1) per window check.
concepts.example:
p = "abc"  →  freq: {a:1, b:1, c:1}
"bca"  →  freq: {a:1, b:1, c:1}  ✓ anagram
"bac"  →  freq: {a:1, b:1, c:1}  ✓ anagram
"abc"  →  freq: {a:1, b:1, c:1}  ✓ anagram
"aab"  →  freq: {a:2, b:1}        ✗ not anagram

easy_medium.text: Fixed sliding window with two frequency arrays, compare on each step
easy_medium.explanation: Window is exactly p.length(). Slide across s, maintaining a window frequency map. Use a 'matches' counter to avoid full map comparison on each step — O(n).

easy_medium.text: Sort each window of size p.length() and compare to sorted p
easy_medium.explanation: O(n × k log k) where k = p.length(). Correct but slow. Sorting a window on every step is unnecessary when frequency comparison is O(1) with the counter trick.

easy_medium.text: Use a HashSet to track unique characters and compare sets
easy_medium.explanation: A set only tells you which characters appear, not how many times. 'aab' and 'abc' have the same character set but are not anagrams.

cards.sayApproach: The window is always exactly p.length() — fixed size. I'll build a frequency array for p (need[]) and maintain a window frequency array (window[]). Instead of comparing all 26 characters each step, I track a 'matches' counter — the number of character positions where need and window agree. When matches reaches 26, the window is an anagram. On each slide I add the new right character and remove the old left, updating matches in O(1). Overall O(n).

lines.text: int[] need = new int[26], win = new int[26];
lines.narration: Constraint: lowercase only — int[26] is safe and faster than HashMap
Fixed window size k; matches counter avoids full array compare
Constraint met for this char — increment matches
Window exceeds size k — shrink left
Requirement: all 26 positions agree — valid anagram window

cards.verbalSummary: Fixed sliding window of size p.length(). The 'matches' counter avoids comparing all 26 frequencies on each step — only the added and removed characters need to update it. Time O(n), space O(1) since the frequency arrays are fixed at size 26.
solRatingCriteria: Identified the fixed window size (p.length()) immediately
Used the matches counter trick instead of full array comparison
Correctly handled both increment and decrement of matches on add/remove
Stated O(1) space for the fixed-size arrays

modifications.question: What if the character set is not limited to lowercase letters?
modifications.approach: Replace int[26] with HashMap<Character, Integer>. The matches counter still works but now tracks the number of characters where need.get(c) == win.get(c). Slightly higher constant factor but same O(n) time.
modifications.codeChunk:
Map<Character, Integer> need = new HashMap<>(), win = new HashMap<>();
// matches tracks keys where win.get(c).equals(need.get(c))

problem.text: Given a binary array nums and an integer k, return the maximum number of consecutive 1s in the array if you can flip at most k 0s.

keywords.explanation: requirement: maximise window length
window validity: count of 0s in window ≤ k
constraint: only 0s and 1s

missingConstraints: Can k be 0 — meaning no flips allowed?
Can the array be empty?

concepts.definition: Flipping at most k zeros is equivalent to finding the longest subarray containing at most k zeros. You don't actually flip anything — you just track how many zeros are in your current window.
concepts.example:
nums = [1,1,0,0,1,1,1,0,1]  k = 2
Window [1,1,0,0,1,1,1] has 2 zeros ✓ — length 7
This is the answer — no flipping needed in code

easy_medium.text: Variable sliding window tracking zero count — shrink left when zeros exceed k
easy_medium.explanation: Expand right; when zerosInWindow > k shrink from left until valid. Track zerosInWindow as a simple counter. O(n) time, O(1) space.

easy_medium.text: Binary search on the answer length, check feasibility for each length
easy_medium.explanation: O(n log n) — works but unnecessary. The sliding window is O(n) and simpler.

easy_medium.text: DP where dp[i] = max consecutive 1s ending at index i with flips used
easy_medium.explanation: Possible but over-engineered. This is a textbook sliding window problem — DP adds unnecessary complexity.

cards.sayApproach: Flipping k zeros is the same as finding the longest window with at most k zeros — I don't actually flip anything. I'll use a variable window with a zero counter. Expand right; if the new element is a zero, increment the counter. When the counter exceeds k, shrink from the left until it's back in range. O(n) time, O(1) space.

lines.narration: Takes array and flip budget k
zeros tracks constraint usage; maxLen tracks requirement
Constraint: track zeros entering the window
Did we remove a zero? Update counter

cards.verbalSummary: Reframe: find the longest window with at most k zeros. Variable sliding window with a zero counter. Expand right, increment counter when we see a zero. Shrink left when counter exceeds k. O(n) time, O(1) space.
solRatingCriteria: Reframed 'flip k zeros' as 'at most k zeros in window'
Used a simple integer counter rather than a data structure
Stated O(n) time and O(1) space
Handled k=0 edge case implicitly (window immediately shrinks past zeros)

modifications.question: Now the array contains integers (not just 0/1) — find the longest subarray where you can replace at most k elements to make all values equal to the most frequent element.
modifications.approach: Track the max frequency in the current window. If (windowSize - maxFreq) > k, shrink. This is the generalised version of Leetcode 424.

problem.text: Given strings s and t, return the minimum window substring of s such that every character in t (including duplicates) is included in the window. Return empty string if no valid window exists.

keywords.explanation: requirement: minimise window length
all chars of t must appear with correct frequency
frequency must match — t='aa' needs two a's in window

missingConstraints: Can s or t be empty?
Is matching case-sensitive?
Can t contain characters not present in s?

concepts.definition: Instead of comparing two full frequency maps on every step (O(|charset|)), track how many character types have been satisfied. When formed == required, the window is valid. This reduces the per-step check to O(1).
concepts.example:
t = "abc"  →  required = 3 (three distinct chars to satisfy)
formed counts how many of those 3 are currently met in the window
When formed == 3 → valid window → try to shrink

options.text: Variable sliding window (expand until valid, shrink to minimise)

cards.sayApproach: Two concerns: how I move through s, and what I track. I need a variable window — expand right until all of t is covered, then shrink left to minimise. For tracking, a HashMap frequency counter is the right tool: I build 'need' from t and maintain 'window' as I slide. The 'formed' counter tells me how many character types are satisfied without comparing full maps. When formed == required I have a valid window — record its length and shrink.

lines.narration: Build frequency map from t — this is what we need
required = distinct char types; formed tracks how many are satisfied
Constraint: this char type is now satisfied
Constraint: char type no longer satisfied
Requirement: return minimum window or empty

cards.verbalSummary: Variable sliding window with a frequency map and a formed counter. Expand right until formed == required (all of t satisfied), then shrink left to find the minimum. The formed counter avoids comparing full maps on each step — O(1) per character processed. Overall O(|s| + |t|) time and space.
solRatingCriteria: Correctly identified variable window (not fixed) because minimum is unknown
Used 'formed' counter to avoid full map comparison per step
Handled the formed decrement correctly when shrinking (only when count drops below need)
Returned empty string for no-valid-window case

modifications.question: What if t can have characters that don't exist in s at all?
modifications.approach: The algorithm handles this naturally — formed never reaches required, so the while loop never executes and we return empty string. Worth mentioning explicitly to the interviewer.
modifications.codeChunk:
// No code change needed — formed never reaches required
// The algorithm returns "" automatically

problem.text: Given a string s, return the number of substrings that contain at least one copy of all the characters 'a', 'b', and 'c'. The string only contains those three characters.

keywords.explanation: count — not just find one, enumerate all
window validity: all three chars present
the window must contain a, b, and c

missingConstraints: Can the string be empty?
Is the string guaranteed to only contain a, b, c?

concepts.definition: A direct approach generates all O(n²) substrings. The key insight: once a window starting at left and ending at right is valid (contains all three chars), every extension of that window to the right is also valid. So the count of valid substrings starting at left is (n - right).
concepts.example:
s = "abcabc"  n = 6
When window [0..2] = "abc" is first valid:
  all substrings starting at 0: [0..2],[0..3],[0..4],[0..5] = (6-2) = 4
Advance left to 1, find next valid right, add (n - right) again

options.text: Shrink-from-left: find the minimum valid window, count extensions
Expand-until-invalid: count all extensions before shrinking

cards.sayApproach: Counting all substrings directly is O(n²). The insight is: once I find the minimum valid window ending at 'right', every extension of that window further right is also valid. So instead of counting each one, I add (n - right) in one step. I advance left to find the next minimum valid window. An int[3] array is cleaner than a HashMap here since we only have three characters. O(n) time.

lines.narration: Constraint: only a/b/c — int[3] is sufficient and faster
Constraint: all three chars present — valid window
Requirement: all extensions from here are valid — count them all
Shrink left to find next minimum valid window

cards.verbalSummary: Key insight: once a window is valid, all extensions to the right are also valid — add (n - right) rather than counting individually. Shrink from left to find the next minimum valid window. O(n) time, O(1) space.
solRatingCriteria: Articulated the 'all extensions are valid' counting insight
Chose int[3] over HashMap for the fixed three-character constraint
Correctly added (n - right) rather than 1 per valid window
Stated O(n) time and O(1) space

modifications.question: Generalise: count substrings containing all k distinct characters of the string.
modifications.approach: Count distinct chars in s first. Then apply the same shrink-and-count approach with a HashMap of size k. The while condition becomes (map.size() == k).

problem.text: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, find the maximum value in each sliding window of size k. Return an array of maximum values.

keywords.explanation: requirement: track max in each window
output one value per window position
fixed window — not variable

missingConstraints: Can k be larger than nums.length?
Can nums contain negative integers?

concepts.definition: A deque (double-ended queue) maintained in decreasing order of values. The front always holds the index of the maximum element in the current window. When a new element is larger than the back of the deque, we pop from the back — those elements can never be the maximum while this larger element is in the window.
concepts.example:
nums = [3,1,2], k=3
Process 3: deque=[0]  (index of 3)
Process 1: 1<3, push  deque=[0,1]
Process 2: 2>1, pop 1. 2<3, push  deque=[0,2]
Front=0 → nums[0]=3 is the max

options.text: Fixed sliding window, process each position once
Variable sliding window, expand and shrink on condition
Divide and conquer over the array
Two-pass: left max and right max arrays

cards.sayApproach: A simple max variable fails here because when the max leaves the window we'd need to scan the whole window again — O(n) per window = O(nk) total. The monotonic deque solves this: maintain a deque of indices in decreasing order of their values. The front always holds the maximum. When adding a new element, pop from the back while the back is smaller — those elements are dominated and can never be future maxima. Pop from the front when the index falls outside the window. O(n) total.

lines.narration: Returns max for each window position
Constraint k: remove indices outside the window
Pattern: maintain decreasing order — pop dominated elements
Back is smaller than current — can never be max while i is here
Requirement: front holds window max — record once window is full

cards.verbalSummary: Monotonic decreasing deque of indices. Each index enters and exits the deque at most once — O(n) total. The front always holds the window maximum. Maintain decreasing order by popping dominated back elements. Pop the front when it falls outside the window. O(n) time, O(k) space for the deque.
solRatingCriteria: Explained why a simple max variable fails (can't recover when max leaves window)
Articulated the monotonic decreasing deque invariant clearly
Stored indices (not values) in the deque for window-expiry check
Stated O(n) time — each element enters/exits deque at most once

modifications.question: Find both the minimum and maximum in each window of size k.
modifications.approach: Maintain two deques: one decreasing (for max) and one increasing (for min). Both run in the same loop. O(n) time.
